items.q: What is the time complexity of binary search?
items.misconception: O(n) - because it searches through items.
items.why: Students conflate 'fast' with O(1). Binary search halves the search space each step, giving logarithmic time - much better than linear, but not constant.

items.q: What is abstraction in computer science?
items.correct: Removing unnecessary detail to focus on essential features of a problem.
items.misconception: Making something abstract art or vague.
items.random_wrong: Writing code that does not work.
items.why: Students borrow the everyday meaning of 'vague'. In CS, abstraction is a precise technique: ignoring details that don't matter for the current task, while keeping what does.

items.q: What is the denary value of binary 10110?
items.why: Students forget to apply place values. Each position is a power of 2 from right (1, 2, 4, 8, 16...). Reading binary as denary is the most common error.
Correct answer: O(log n). “O(1)” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Best possible time for a comparison-based sort?
items.why: It's O(n log n). “O(n)” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many comparisons does an O(n) linear search worst-case take on 100 items?
items.why: Exact answer: 100. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
